name: Present Tense (-er verbs)
summary: Conjugate regular -er verbs, by far the largest and most useful verb group.
explanation:
Most French verbs end in -er (parler, to speak; aimer, to like). To conjugate them, drop the -er to get the stem (parl-) and add the present-tense endings.

The endings are: -e (je), -es (tu), -e (il/elle), -ons (nous), -ez (vous), -ent (ils/elles). The je, tu, il, and ils/elles endings are all silent, so parle, parles, and parlent sound identical.

French present tense covers both English 'I speak' and 'I am speaking', so je parle means both.
pattern: stem + e / es / e / ons / ez / ent
commonMistakes: Pronouncing the silent endings. parle, parles, and parlent sound the same.
Using an English continuous form. There is no 'je suis parler'; just say je parle.
Forgetting the -ons for nous and -ez for vous, the only clearly audible endings.
Mixing up il/elle (-e) with ils/elles (-ent), which look different but sound alike.
Dropping the subject pronoun. Unlike Spanish, French keeps je, tu, il, etc.
tip: Learn the six endings as a chant: e, es, e, ons, ez, ent. They fit thousands of -er verbs.
